title: Frontend Web Development
description: Build what users see and interact with in the browser. This track covers everything from your first webpage to modern JavaScript frameworks. Perfect first track for total beginners.

steps.title: HTML — Structure of the Web
steps.description: HTML is the skeleton of every webpage. You'll learn how to create headings, paragraphs, links, images, forms, and lists. This is literally step 1 of the entire web — no exceptions.

steps.title: CSS — Styling & Layout
steps.description: CSS controls how your HTML looks — colors, fonts, spacing, and layout. Learn Flexbox and Grid — they are the two layout systems every frontend developer uses daily. Don't skip them.

steps.title: JavaScript — Making Pages Interactive
steps.description: JavaScript brings your pages to life. Learn variables, functions, arrays, events, and DOM manipulation. This is the programming language of the web — every frontend job requires it.

steps.title: Git & Version Control
steps.description: Git lets you save snapshots of your code and collaborate with others. You'll use it at every job, in every project. Learn: init, add, commit, push, pull, branches, and merge.

steps.title: Responsive Design & Mobile-First
steps.description: Your sites must work on phones, tablets, and desktops. Learn media queries, viewport units, and how to design mobile-first. This is expected in every frontend role.

steps.title: CSS Frameworks (Tailwind or Bootstrap)
steps.description: These utility toolkits let you build UIs faster by using pre-built class names instead of writing all CSS from scratch. Learn vanilla CSS first — then pick these up once you're comfortable.

steps.title: A JavaScript Framework (React or Vue)
steps.description: Modern frontend apps are built with frameworks. React is the most in-demand right now, followed by Vue. These tools let you build complex, fast UIs with reusable components. Only learn after mastering vanilla JS.

steps.title: TypeScript
steps.description: TypeScript adds static types to JavaScript, catching bugs before your code even runs. Most large companies use it. Learn it after you're confident with JavaScript — not before.

steps.title: Build & Deploy Your First Project
steps.description: The best way to learn is to build. Create a portfolio website, a to-do app, or a weather app and deploy it free on GitHub Pages, Vercel, or Netlify. This becomes your first portfolio piece.

title: Backend Development
description: Build the engines that power applications — databases, APIs, authentication, and server logic. Backend is invisible to users but runs everything behind the scenes.

steps.title: Pick a Backend Language (Python or JavaScript/Node)
steps.description: For beginners: Python is the most readable and forgiving. For those who already know JavaScript: Node.js lets you use the same language on both sides. Pick one and stick with it — don't try to learn both at once.

steps.title: Git & Version Control
steps.description: Same as frontend — you'll use Git everywhere. Learn the fundamentals early before your codebase gets complex.

steps.title: How the Web Works (HTTP, APIs, REST)
steps.description: Learn what happens when you open a website — DNS, HTTP requests, status codes (200, 404, 500). Then understand APIs: how clients and servers communicate using JSON over HTTP. This is foundational for all backend work.

steps.title: SQL & Databases
steps.description: Data is stored in databases. Learn SQL to query, insert, update, and delete data. Start with SQLite or PostgreSQL. Understand tables, relationships, and joins — they are the bread and butter of backend work.

steps.title: Build a Web Framework App (Express, Flask, or Django)
steps.description: Frameworks make it easier to build servers. Flask (Python, minimal) or Express (Node.js) are great starting points. Django (Python, full-featured) is better for larger projects. Build a CRUD app: Create, Read, Update, Delete records via a REST API.

steps.title: Authentication & Security Basics
steps.description: Learn JWT tokens, sessions, OAuth (login with Google/GitHub), password hashing with bcrypt, and basic input validation. Never trust user input — learn why and how to protect against SQL injection and XSS.

steps.title: NoSQL Databases (MongoDB)
steps.description: MongoDB stores data as JSON-like documents instead of tables. Very popular in the Node.js ecosystem. Learn it after you understand SQL — it'll make more sense with context.

title: Fullstack Development
description: The combination of Frontend + Backend. You handle both what users see and the server logic behind it. The most versatile — and most in-demand — role for individual developers and startups.

steps.title: Complete the Frontend Roadmap first
steps.description: Master HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and at least the basics of a framework (React or Vue) before attempting fullstack work. Trying to learn both sides at once without a foundation is the #1 reason beginners get overwhelmed and quit.

steps.title: Complete the Backend Roadmap second
steps.description: Learn server-side programming, databases, REST APIs, and authentication. The backend track takes you through all of this systematically.

steps.title: Connect Frontend to Your Own Backend API
steps.description: Build a project where a React/Vue frontend makes fetch requests to your own Express/Flask backend, which reads and writes to a database. This single project will teach you more than months of tutorials.

steps.title: Learn a Fullstack Framework (Next.js or Nuxt)
steps.description: Next.js (React) and Nuxt (Vue) are meta-frameworks that handle both the frontend and backend in one project with file-based routing, server-side rendering, and built-in API routes. Very popular in the industry.

title: Python / Data Science Track
description: Use Python to analyze data, build machine learning models, and automate tasks. A great track for those interested in AI, research, finance, biology, or automation rather than traditional app development.

steps.title: Python Fundamentals
steps.description: Variables, data types, loops, functions, lists, dictionaries, and file I/O. Python's syntax is one of the most readable — it reads almost like English. This is the foundation for everything in this track.

steps.title: Mathematics for Data Science (Basic)
steps.description: You don't need a math degree, but learn the basics: statistics (mean, median, standard deviation), probability, and linear algebra (vectors, matrices). Khan Academy covers all of this for free.

steps.title: NumPy & Pandas
steps.description: NumPy handles fast numerical arrays. Pandas handles tabular data (like Excel spreadsheets in Python). Together they are the foundation of every data science workflow — you'll use them constantly.

steps.title: Data Visualization (Matplotlib & Seaborn)
steps.description: Turn raw numbers into charts, graphs, and plots. Being able to visualize data is how you find patterns and communicate insights. These two libraries cover 90% of what you'll ever need.

steps.title: Scikit-learn (Machine Learning Basics)
steps.description: Scikit-learn makes it easy to apply common ML algorithms (linear regression, decision trees, clustering) without writing them from scratch. Start here before touching deep learning.

title: DevOps Track
description: Deploy, automate, and scale applications. DevOps engineers bridge development and operations — they make sure code runs reliably in production. Best approached after learning the basics of backend development.

steps.title: Linux & Command Line
steps.description: Almost all servers run Linux. Learn how to navigate the filesystem, manage files, set permissions, and write bash scripts. This is non-negotiable for DevOps — every other step builds on it.

steps.title: Git & Advanced Version Control
steps.description: Beyond basic Git — learn branching strategies (GitFlow), rebasing, cherry-picking, and GitHub Actions for automated workflows triggered on every code push.

steps.title: Docker & Containers
steps.description: Docker packages your app and all its dependencies into a container — a box that runs the same on any machine. It solves the "works on my machine" problem. Learn: images, containers, Dockerfiles, and Docker Compose.

steps.title: CI/CD Pipelines
steps.description: CI/CD (Continuous Integration/Deployment) automatically builds, tests, and deploys your app when you push code. Use GitHub Actions as your starting point — it's free for public repos and integrates directly with GitHub.

steps.title: Cloud Platforms (AWS, Azure, or GCP)
steps.description: Learn one cloud provider's basics: VMs, object storage (S3), databases, and networking. AWS has the largest market share and most job listings — but all three providers offer free tiers to practice on.
